description:
Advances: mutagen.mutation_pipeline

The `.25` AST/perf epic shipped (S1-S6) but the bench harness (S7,
mutagen-wrd.25.7) measured **1.66×** wall-clock speedup on the
`wrd25_200sites` fixture, below the spec's 2-4× lower bound.

## Baseline numbers (Elixir 1.19.5 / OTP 28, scope `lib/arith_dense.ex`)

| metric        | before (`978a995`) | after (`78b022f`) |
|---------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| sites/run     | 87                 | 87                |
| wall_ms       | 2179.53            | 1314.00           |
| per_site_us   | 25052.05           | 15103.49          |

Reproduce: `mix run priv/helper_scripts/bench_ast_perf.exs --
--baseline /tmp/bench_before.json` (on `978a995`), then on the
current commit `mix run priv/helper_scripts/bench_ast_perf.exs --
--compare /tmp/bench_before.json`.

## Likely root causes

The bench fixture has small file ASTs (~40 lines) — S5's batched
prewalk wins ~O(sites × file_ast_size) which under-shows on
small files. On a real codebase with multi-thousand-line scope
files the win should compound. Two candidates:

1. **Mutators.normalize/1** (out of scope per `.25`) was flagged
   as a hot path in the consolidated review. Profiling against the
   bench fixture should confirm or rule out.
2. **Per-site ExUnit.run/0 + :cover instrumentation boot.** Each
   mutation pays the full ExUnit startup cost; a longer-lived test
   runner process (or ExUnit.Server priming) could amortise this
   across the per-site loop. Big change, deferred to `.30` parallel
   work or a dedicated epic.

## Verification

- Profile the post-.25 bench run to identify where the remaining
  ~85% of per-site time goes (`:fprof` or `:eprof`).
- Decide whether normalize/1 belongs in a follow-up epic or is
  incidental.
- Document the findings (even if the conclusion is "the bench
  fixture isn't representative of real codebases").
title: Triage .25 speedup shortfall to 2-4x target
